_note: Imported from socratic-planning/exams/cbse10/maths/jemh101 (single source of truth). One gate per concept. expected/answer are SERVER-ONLY at runtime — never serialise to the client.

concepts.title: Recognise coprime integers (common factor only 1) and the prime/composite distinction
concepts.brief: Coprime numbers share no common factor other than 1; factorisation targets composites and produces primes.
concepts.explanation: The irrationality proofs rely on writing a fraction in coprime form: a and b are coprime means their only common factor is 1. The same prime/composite vocabulary underlies factorisation (composites factor into primes). Without firm grasp of these terms the rules are unusable.
keyMoves: Recognise coprime pairs (only common factor is 1, i.e. HCF = 1)
Reduce a fraction to coprime numerator and denominator
Use 'product of primes' as the goal of factorising a composite
misconceptions: Calling 1 a prime, or treating it as composite

concepts.title: Factorise a composite number into a product of primes
concepts.brief: Break a positive integer into prime factors using a factor tree and write it as a product of powers of primes in ascending order.
concepts.explanation: The chapter demonstrates the factor-tree procedure on 32760, repeatedly splitting off the smallest prime factor until only primes remain, then collecting repeated primes into powers written in ascending order. This is the workhorse subskill behind HCF, LCM, and divisibility arguments.
keyMoves: Divide out the smallest prime repeatedly via a factor tree
Continue until every leaf is prime
Combine repeated primes into powers and order ascending
misconceptions: Stopping before all factors are prime (leaving a composite factor)
Listing primes in arbitrary order or losing a repeated factor's exponent

concepts.title: State the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic (existence + uniqueness)
concepts.brief: Know that every composite number factorises as a product of primes, and that this factorisation is unique apart from order.
concepts.explanation: The theorem has two halves: existence (a factorisation exists) and uniqueness (only one, up to ordering). The uniqueness half is the part repeatedly invoked later to argue which primes can or cannot appear in a factorisation.
keyMoves: State existence: every composite is a product of primes
State uniqueness: only one factorisation, ignoring order
Recognise uniqueness as the deductive tool, not just existence
misconceptions: Quoting only existence and dropping uniqueness
Thinking different orderings count as different factorisations

gates.prompt: Express 32760 as a product of powers of primes (write exponents, e.g. 2^3 * 3^2 * 5 * 7 * 13).
gates.quote: 32760 = 2³ × 3² × 5 × 7 × 13 as a product of powers of primes.

gates.prompt: The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic states that every composite number can be factorised as a product of primes, and that this factorisation is: A) never unique  B) unique apart from the order of factors  C) unique only for even numbers  D) unique including the order of factors

expected.correct: unique apart from the order of factors
options: unique apart from the order of factors
unique including the order of factors

gates.answer: unique apart from the order of factors
gates.quote: Every composite number can be expressed (factorised) as a product of primes, and this factorisation is unique, apart from the order in which the prime factors occur.

gates.prompt: In the proof that √2 is irrational, the fraction is reduced so that a and b are coprime. Two integers being coprime means: A) both a and b are prime numbers  B) their only common factor is 1 (HCF = 1)  C) a and b are both even  D) a divides b

options: both a and b are prime numbers
a and b are both even

gates.quote: Then, we divide by the common factor to get √2 = a/b, where a and b are coprime.
